refined_fr.refined_fr: FR-NGHE-004.1: Maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a numerical availability target, and (2) doing so within a defined measurement window. Splitting these into atomic FRs allows independent design decisions and verification strategies. The refined FR focuses on the primary functional obligation—sustaining availability performance—while treating the measurement period as a constraint context rather than a functional requirement. This formulation is solution-neutral: it does not prescribe redundancy, predictive maintenance, or any specific DP; it only states the availability outcome required.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (total time − unplanned downtime) / total time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold at the end of the evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: Maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: Establish and communicate the availability measurement methodology and evaluation period to all stakeholders.
FR-NGHE-004.3: Provide visibility into availability performance (uptime/downtime events) to Site Manager and Project Manager during the evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP-NGHE-004.1a: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity during component degradation.
DP-NGHE-004.1b: Design preventive maintenance scheduling and condition-monitoring protocols to detect and replace wear-critical components before failure, reducing unplanned downtime.
DP-NGHE-004.1c: Architect modular, hot-swappable component design with standardized interfaces to enable rapid field replacement without system shutdown.
DP-NGHE-004.1d: Establish operational resilience through graceful degradation modes that allow partial functionality continuation when non-critical subsystems fail.
DP-NGHE-004.1e: Implement comprehensive fault detection and diagnostic logging to enable rapid root-cause analysis and minimize mean-time-to-repair (MTTR).
DP-NGHE-004.1f: Allocate operational buffer capacity (thermal, electrical, mechanical headroom) to reduce stress-induced failures and extend component life under nominal conditions.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-NGHE-004.1: Composite availability strategy combining (1) redundancy for critical failure modes, (2) preventive maintenance and condition monitoring to extend component life, (3) modular hot-swappable design for rapid repair, (4) graceful degradation to maintain partial service, and (5) comprehensive diagnostics to minimize MTTR. Buffer capacity allocation reduces stress-induced failures.

potential_sources: Redundancy architecture (DP-004.1a) couples tightly with control logic (FR-NGHE-003 or equivalent) and power distribution design; requires explicit interface specification to avoid circular dependencies.
Preventive maintenance scheduling (DP-004.1b) couples with operational planning and worksite safety policies; must define clear handoff boundaries between system design and operational procedures.
Hot-swappable modularity (DP-004.1c) couples with mechanical/electrical interface standardization; risk of over-constraining downstream design if interfaces are too rigid.
Graceful degradation (DP-004.1d) couples with functional mode definitions (FR-NGHE-002 or equivalent); requires explicit specification of which functions remain available in degraded states.
Diagnostic logging (DP-004.1e) couples with data storage and communication subsystems; must isolate logging from critical real-time paths to avoid availability regression.
coupling_check.notes: The composite DP is solution-neutral and logically sound, but its realization requires careful decomposition into independent sub-DPs with clear ownership boundaries. Coupling is manageable if interface contracts and degradation modes are explicitly defined early. Risk: attempting to implement all sub-strategies simultaneously without phased validation may introduce unintended interactions.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined target threshold (≥0.97) is achievable with reasonable cost and complexity; if target is significantly higher (e.g., 0.999), redundancy and diagnostics alone may be insufficient without architectural redesign.
ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough (weeks to months) to capture representative failure modes; short evaluation windows may not expose latent defects or seasonal stress patterns.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are stable and compatible with proposed maintenance and failover procedures; changes to operational constraints post-design could invalidate availability targets.

RISK: Redundancy introduces complexity and potential for common-mode failures if redundant subsystems share environmental stressors (e.g., temperature, vibration, contamination); mitigation requires diversity in redundant paths.
RISK: Preventive maintenance scheduling assumes accurate failure prediction models; if component wear is unpredictable or highly variable, maintenance intervals may be ineffective or excessive.
RISK: Hot-swappable design increases component cost and interface complexity; if field replacement is rare, modularity overhead may not justify the investment.
RISK: Graceful degradation requires explicit definition of acceptable partial-function states; ambiguity here can lead to customer dissatisfaction or safety violations.
RISK: Diagnostic logging adds computational and storage overhead; if not carefully isolated, logging can degrade real-time performance and paradoxically reduce availability.

RISK: Availability measurement methodology (uptime vs. downtime, scheduled vs. unscheduled, customer-perceived vs. system-reported) must be agreed in advance; disputes over measurement can invalidate acceptance criteria.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR-NGHE-004.1 is well-formed and the composite DP is logically sound. Recommend: (1) Decompose the composite DP into independent sub-DPs (redundancy, maintenance, modularity, degradation, diagnostics, buffering) with explicit ownership and interface contracts to enable parallel design and testing. (2) Define acceptance criteria operationally: specify measurement methodology (e.g., uptime percentage, MTBF, MTTR targets), evaluation period duration, and failure classification (scheduled vs. unscheduled). (3) Establish a failure-mode and effects analysis (FMEA) to validate that proposed redundancy and degradation modes actually achieve the 0.97 target; avoid over-design. (4) Clarify the boundary between system design (DP) and operational procedures (worksite safety plan); specify which availability levers are design-controlled vs. operationally-controlled. (5) Add a sub-FR for 'Availability Measurement and Reporting' to ensure transparent, auditable tracking of the metric.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: System-level availability architecture encompassing redundancy strategy, maintenance coordination, modular design governance, graceful degradation logic, and diagnostic framework across all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound condition monitoring data from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to feed preventive maintenance and diagnostics logic.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound graceful degradation directives to reduce operational stress; inbound task requests to assess feasibility under current redundancy/degradation state.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound alerts when critical failure modes are detected or redundancy thresholds breached; coordinates with Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Inbound/outbound for distributed diagnostics and remote maintenance coordination across Vehicle and Teleoperation nodes.

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest containment. DP-NGHE-004.1 is fundamentally a system-level availability strategy that spans redundancy governance, maintenance policy, modular design principles, and graceful degradation—all architectural concerns that belong at the solution level. Redundancy and hot-swap design are enforced across Vehicle and Teleoperation; diagnostics and MTTR optimization are cross-system. Minor diffusion: actual redundant hardware/software instances reside in Vehicle and Teleoperation, but the strategy and coordination logic are owned here.

Weak containment. Vehicle would own physical redundancy (e.g., dual motors, sensor arrays) and hot-swappable components, but the composite strategy also requires Teleoperation redundancy (communication links, control channels), Safety Services coordination (alert routing), and system-level graceful degradation logic. Preventive maintenance scheduling and diagnostics must span multiple subsystems. High diffusion across Teleoperation and Safety Services.

Weak containment. Teleoperation would own communication redundancy and link-level hot-swap strategies, but the DP also requires Vehicle-side redundancy (mechanical, sensor), Safety Services integration (alert handling), and system-level graceful degradation. Condition monitoring and MTTR optimization span Vehicle telemetry. High diffusion.

Poor containment. Safety Services would own alert generation and safety-critical diagnostics, but the DP includes redundancy design, maintenance scheduling, modular hot-swap architecture, and graceful degradation—none of which are primarily safety-service concerns. The DP is broader than safety; it is availability-centric. High diffusion across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP-NGHE-004.1 is a composite availability strategy that defines system-level policies and coordination mechanisms for redundancy, maintenance, modularity, degradation, and diagnostics. These are architectural concerns that transcend individual subsystems. The NGHE Solution module is the natural owner because it governs the integration and orchestration of these strategies across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Specific implementations (e.g., dual motors in Vehicle, redundant communication in Teleoperation) are owned by their respective subsystems, but the strategy, policy, and cross-system coordination belong at the solution level.
containment_risks: Risk: Diffusion of redundancy design decisions. If Vehicle and Teleoperation independently choose redundancy architectures without coordination, the composite strategy may become incoherent. Mitigation: NGHE Solution must define redundancy design patterns and enforce them via interface contracts.

Risk: Maintenance scheduling and condition monitoring require real-time data from Vehicle and Teleoperation. If these subsystems do not expose sufficient telemetry, the preventive maintenance logic cannot function. Mitigation: Telemetry interface must be contractually rich enough to support condition-based maintenance decisions.

Risk: Graceful degradation logic may conflict with Safety Services constraints. If Safety Services mandates immediate shutdown on certain faults, graceful degradation may be overridden. Mitigation: NGHE Solution must coordinate with Safety Services to define safe degradation modes.

Risk: MTTR optimization requires rapid diagnostics and repair procedures. If Vehicle and Teleoperation do not support modular hot-swap design, MTTR cannot be minimized. Mitigation: Modular design must be enforced as a design principle across subsystems.

Risk: Distributed diagnostics across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services may create latency or inconsistency. Mitigation: Network Link interface must support low-latency diagnostic data exchange.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision recommended. DP-NGHE-004.1 is appropriately scoped as a system-level availability strategy and maps cleanly to NGHE Solution as the owning module. The DP is not implementation-specific; it defines logical strategies (redundancy, maintenance, modularity, degradation, diagnostics) that are realized across multiple subsystems. The key to successful containment is ensuring that Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services implement their respective portions of the strategy in compliance with NGHE Solution's architectural policies and interface contracts.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability target (≥0.97) is measurable and traceable to NGHE Solution as the owning module. Verification requires instrumentation of redundancy activation events, maintenance scheduling compliance, graceful degradation transitions, and MTTR metrics across Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems. The Telemetry and Network Link interfaces provide the necessary data channels. Feasibility is high provided subsystems expose required telemetry and diagnostic hooks.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-004.1 is clearly allocated to NGHE Solution (containment_score 0.92). The DP defines system-level policies and coordination mechanisms; specific implementations (redundant hardware in Vehicle, communication links in Teleoperation) are allocated to their respective subsystems but governed by NGHE Solution's architectural contracts. Allocation is clear and non-ambiguous.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-NGHE-004.1 directly addresses FR-NGHE-004.1. The composite strategy (redundancy, preventive maintenance, modular hot-swap, graceful degradation, diagnostics) is logically sound and covers all major availability levers. The DP is solution-agnostic (does not prescribe specific hardware or algorithms) and is appropriately pitched at the design domain. Traceability to acceptance criterion (≥0.97 availability) is established through MTTR minimization, stress reduction via buffer capacity, and redundancy for critical failure modes.

diffusion_risks: Redundancy design decisions may diffuse across Vehicle and Teleoperation if architectural patterns are not enforced. Mitigation: NGHE Solution must publish redundancy design guidelines and enforce via interface contracts (e.g., mandatory dual-channel telemetry, hot-swap interface specifications).

Preventive maintenance logic depends on telemetry richness from Vehicle and Teleoperation. If subsystems do not expose sufficient condition-monitoring data (e.g., component wear indicators, thermal profiles), the DP cannot be realized. Mitigation: Telemetry interface must include contractual requirements for condition-monitoring data granularity and update frequency.

Graceful degradation directives (Tasking interface, outbound) may conflict with Safety Services constraints. If Safety Services mandates hard shutdown on certain faults, graceful degradation may be preempted. Mitigation: NGHE Solution must coordinate with Safety Services to define safe degradation modes and establish priority rules (e.g., safety override availability).

Distributed diagnostics across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services may introduce latency or data inconsistency, degrading MTTR optimization. Mitigation: Network Link interface must specify diagnostic data exchange latency bounds and consistency guarantees (e.g., eventual consistency with bounded staleness).

Modular hot-swap design is a DP principle but realization depends on Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystem architecture. If subsystems do not support modular design (e.g., tightly coupled components, non-standard interfaces), hot-swap cannot be achieved. Mitigation: Modular design must be enforced as a design principle in subsystem DPs; NGHE Solution must define standard hot-swap interface patterns.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: DP-NGHE-004.1 is appropriately contained within NGHE Solution (0.92 containment score). The DP is fundamentally a system-level availability strategy that orchestrates redundancy governance, maintenance policy, modular design principles, and graceful degradation across subsystems. Minor diffusion (0.08) is acceptable because actual redundant instances (hardware, communication links) reside in Vehicle and Teleoperation, but the strategy and coordination logic are owned by NGHE Solution. The key to successful containment is ensuring that subsystem DPs (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) implement their portions of the strategy in compliance with NGHE Solution's architectural policies and interface contracts. No DP reallocation is recommended.

recommended_revisions: Strengthen Telemetry interface contract: Specify minimum condition-monitoring data requirements (e.g., component health indicators, thermal/vibration profiles, failure prediction signals) and update frequency to support preventive maintenance logic. Add acceptance criterion: 'Telemetry interface shall provide condition data with latency ≤ 5 seconds to enable real-time maintenance decisions.'

Define redundancy design patterns: NGHE Solution should publish architectural patterns for redundancy (e.g., active-active, active-passive, N+1) and mandate their use in Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystem DPs. Add design rule: 'All critical failure modes shall be covered by at least one redundancy pattern per NGHE Solution architecture guide.'

Establish graceful degradation coordination protocol: Define safe degradation modes in collaboration with Safety Services. Add interface rule: 'Graceful degradation directives shall be validated against Safety Services constraints before execution; Safety Services may override degradation if safety threshold is breached.'

Specify diagnostic data exchange latency bounds: Network Link interface should guarantee low-latency diagnostic data exchange to support MTTR optimization. Add acceptance criterion: 'Diagnostic data exchange latency shall not exceed 2 seconds (p95) to enable rapid fault isolation and repair.'

Enforce modular design in subsystem DPs: Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystem DPs should explicitly require modular, hot-swappable component design. Add design rule: 'All components subject to preventive maintenance shall support hot-swap via standard interface patterns defined by NGHE Solution.'

Add availability monitoring and reporting: NGHE Solution should include a monitoring module that tracks redundancy activation events, maintenance scheduling compliance, graceful degradation transitions, and MTTR metrics. Add acceptance criterion: 'Availability monitoring shall report system availability with granularity ≤ 1 hour and accuracy ≥ 99% to enable continuous verification against ≥0.97 target.'

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence (containment 0.92, verification feasibility confirmed). However, the gate analysis reveals six material diffusion risks and five recommended revisions that are architectural in nature and require explicit sub-FR decomposition to ensure traceability, ownership clarity, and risk mitigation. The high safety criticality of this system demands that availability strategy be decomposed into verifiable, independently owned sub-requirements rather than relying on interface contracts alone. Proceeding to next-level decomposition will: (1) make diffusion risks explicit as sub-FR allocation decisions, (2) establish clear ownership boundaries for redundancy governance, maintenance policy, graceful degradation coordination, and diagnostics, (3) create measurable acceptance criteria for each architectural concern, and (4) enable safety-critical verification at finer granularity. The recommended revisions (e.g., 'Strengthen Telemetry interface contract', 'Define redundancy design patterns', 'Establish graceful degradation coordination protocol') are best realized as sub-FRs with dedicated DPs and IMs.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1.1: Govern redundancy design patterns and enforcement across Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to ensure all critical failure modes are covered by at least one redundancy strategy (active-active, active-passive, N+1) and to prevent ad-hoc diffusion of redundancy logic.

FR-NGHE-004.1.2: Establish preventive maintenance policy and condition-monitoring data requirements to enable predictive maintenance scheduling and reduce unplanned downtime; ensure Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems expose sufficient condition-monitoring telemetry (component health, thermal/vibration profiles, failure prediction signals) with latency ≤ 5 seconds.

FR-NGHE-004.1.3: Coordinate graceful degradation directives with Safety Services constraints to define safe degradation modes, establish priority rules (e.g., safety override availability), and prevent conflicts between availability optimization and safety thresholds.

FR-NGHE-004.1.4: Specify diagnostic data exchange latency bounds and consistency guarantees across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services to support MTTR optimization; ensure diagnostic data exchange latency does not exceed 2 seconds (p95) to enable rapid fault isolation and repair.

FR-NGHE-004.1.5: Enforce modular, hot-swappable component design in Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems via standard interface patterns to enable rapid component replacement and minimize maintenance downtime.

FR-NGHE-004.1.6: Monitor and report system availability metrics (redundancy activation events, maintenance scheduling compliance, graceful degradation transitions, MTTR) with granularity ≤ 1 hour and accuracy ≥ 99% to enable continuous verification against ≥0.97 availability target and support closed-loop availability optimization.

revise.why: No revisions to current-level FR, DP, or IM are required. The gates confirm that FR-NGHE-004.1, DP-NGHE-004.1, and IM allocation are sound. Revisions recommended by the gate analysis (e.g., strengthen Telemetry interface, define redundancy patterns, establish graceful degradation protocol) are best addressed through sub-FR decomposition rather than rework of the current level.

assumptions: Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems will implement their portions of the availability strategy in compliance with NGHE Solution's architectural policies and interface contracts.
Safety Services will cooperate in defining safe graceful degradation modes and will not impose hard shutdown mandates that preempt all availability optimization.
Telemetry and Network Link interfaces will be enhanced to support condition-monitoring data and low-latency diagnostic exchange as specified in sub-FR acceptance criteria.
Modular design principles will be enforced as a design rule in Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystem DPs.
NGHE Solution has sufficient architectural authority to publish and enforce redundancy design patterns, hot-swap interface standards, and graceful degradation coordination protocols.
open_risks: Redundancy design decisions may diffuse across Vehicle and Teleoperation if architectural patterns are not enforced; mitigation requires sub-FR-NGHE-004.1.1 to establish clear governance and design rules.

Preventive maintenance logic depends on telemetry richness from subsystems; if condition-monitoring data is insufficient, DP cannot be realized; mitigation requires sub-FR-NGHE-004.1.2 to specify contractual telemetry requirements.
Graceful degradation directives may conflict with Safety Services constraints; mitigation requires sub-FR-NGHE-004.1.3 to establish coordination protocol and priority rules.
Distributed diagnostics may introduce latency or data inconsistency, degrading MTTR optimization; mitigation requires sub-FR-NGHE-004.1.4 to specify latency bounds and consistency guarantees.

Modular hot-swap design depends on subsystem architecture; if subsystems do not support modular design, hot-swap cannot be achieved; mitigation requires sub-FR-NGHE-004.1.5 to enforce modular design principles.
Availability monitoring and reporting infrastructure may not exist; mitigation requires sub-FR-NGHE-004.1.6 to establish monitoring module and acceptance criteria.
evidence_needed: Confirmation from Vehicle subsystem that redundancy design patterns can be implemented and that condition-monitoring telemetry can be exposed with latency ≤ 5 seconds.
Confirmation from Teleoperation subsystem that redundancy design patterns can be implemented and that condition-monitoring telemetry can be exposed with latency ≤ 5 seconds.
Confirmation from Safety Services that graceful degradation coordination protocol is feasible and that safe degradation modes can be defined without compromising safety thresholds.
Confirmation from Network Link interface owner that diagnostic data exchange latency ≤ 2 seconds (p95) is achievable.
Confirmation from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems that modular, hot-swappable component design can be enforced via standard interface patterns.
Confirmation that availability monitoring and reporting infrastructure can be implemented with granularity ≤ 1 hour and accuracy ≥ 99%.
Safety analysis (FMEA, FTA) confirming that the six sub-FRs and their DPs do not introduce new safety risks or violate safety-critical constraints.
